coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)/Co3O4 composite material as an efficient bi-functional electrocatalyst for oxygen evolution and oxygen reduction reactions
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2017 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Muthurasu, A.
Sheen Mers, S.V.
Ganesh, V.
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a facile development of a bi-functional electrocatalyst for oxygen evolution reaction (OER) and oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) is reported. A composite material comprising of tiny particles of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) embedded into cobalt oxide (Co3O4) flakes is prepared by sodium borohydride reduction method and followed by annealing at 600 °C under inert atmosphere. Structural, morphological and crystalline features are analyzed using FESEM, TEM, HRTEM, XRD and XPS studies. Moreover, optical and fluorescence properties of N-GQDs are studied using UV–visible and fluorescence spectroscopic techniques. These studies clearly reveal and confirm the formation of a composite material. Further electrochemical characteristics toward OER and ORR are investigated by using linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) techniques. Compared to the individual entities of pure Co3O4 and N-GQDs alone, the electrocatalytic activity of N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite material is significantly higher towards ORR. Similarly, the same composite material is also used as an electrocatalyst for OER in 0.1 M KOH aqueous electrolyte and it exhibits a lower overpotential of 330 mV to obtain a current density of 10 mA/cm2 along with higher electrocatalytic activity and the reason is mainly attributed to the synergistic effect between N-GQDs and Co3O4. Thus, N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite material is demonstrated to be a high performance bi-functional electrocatalyst for ORR and OER.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bi-functional
Cobalt oxide
Composite material
Electrocatalyst
Graphene quantum dots
Oxygen evolution reaction and oxygen reduction reaction

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Special Section on Eleventh International Symposium on Advances in Electrochemical Science and Technology (iSAEST-11), 08-10 December 2016, Chennai, India

xocs:meta.xocs:copyright-line: © 2017 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical and microscopic studies of N-GQDs
Structural and morphological analyses of N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite
XRD and FTIR studies of N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite
Electrocatalytic activity of N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite material towards OER
Electrocatalytic activity of N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite towards ORR

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2017 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) image of N-GQDs and the zoomed version displaying the formation of lattice fringe plane corresponding to N-GQDs is shown as an inset. (B) UV–visible spectrum of N-GQDs (a) and PL emission spectra of N-GQDs (b) recorded at various excitation wavelengths ranging from 360 nm (a) to 470 nm (l). Inset: Photographic image of N-GQDs dispersed in aqueous solution taken under UV light (365 nm) exposure.

Field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) images of N-GQDs/Co3O4, at low magnification (A) and at high magnification (B). (C) HRTEM image of N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite and the corresponding regions marked with hexagon and circles indicate the presence of Co3O4 flakes and N-GQDs particles respectively. Inset image shows the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern and (D) displays the lattice fringe spacing of the resultant N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite obtained from HRTEM studies.
(A) XRD pattern of N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite and the corresponding JCPDS card No. 74-1657. (B) FTIR spectra of (a) N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite, (b) Co3O4 and (c) N-GQDs respectively.
XPS spectra of N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite displaying the high-resolution spectra of (A) N 1s, (B) C 1s, (C) Co 2p and (D) O 1s respectively.

(A) LSV curves of IrO2 (a), N-GQDs/Co3O4 (b), Co3O4 (c), Pt/C (d), N-GQDs (e) and bare GC (f) electrodes in 0.1 M KOH aqueous solution at a scan rate of 5 mV/s and inset graph is zoomed version of N-GQDs (d) and bare GC electrode (f) respectively. (B) Tafel plots of N-GQDs/Co3O4 (a), Co3O4 (b), N-GQDs (c) and bare GC (d) electrodes obtained from CA studies carried out at various applied potential values. (C) CA plots of N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite material measured at various applied potential values from 330 mV (a) to 520 mV (s) at a potential interval of 10 mV and the arrow indicates the direction of increasing current with various applied potential values. (D) CA curve corresponding to N-GQDs/Co3O4 at an applied potential of 1.53 V vs. RHE in 0.1 M KOH aqueous electrolyte.

(A) CV curves of N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite material in N2 (a) and O2 (b) saturated 0.1 M KOH aqueous solution at a scan rate of 10 mV/s. (B) CVs recorded for ORR using N-GQDs (a), bare GC (b), Co3O4 (c) and N-GQDs/Co3O4 (d) in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH electrolyte at a scan rate of 10 mV/s. (C) Rotating-disk voltammograms of N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite in O2 saturated 0.1 M KOH aqueous solution with a sweep rate of 10 mV/s at different rotation rates 0 (a), 100 (b), 400 (c), 900 (d), 1600 (e) and 2500 (f) rpm respectively. Arrow indicates the direction of increasing rotation rates. (D) Corresponding Koutecky – Levich (KL) plots measured at different potential values from −0.4 V to −1 V vs. Ag/AgCl. Inset shows the KL plots measured at the lower potential ranges from −0.2 V to −0.35 V vs. Ag/AgCl. These data points are obtained from figure (C).
Pictorial illustration of the preparation of N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite material and its use as a bi-functional electrocatalyst for OER and ORR.
Comparison of lattice spacing (“d” values) determined from the JCPDS file with that of SAED pattern of N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)/Co3O4 composite material as an efficient bi-functional electrocatalyst for oxygen evolution and oxygen reduction reactions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electrodics and Electrocatalysis (EEC) Division, CSIR – Central Electrochemical Research Institute (CSIR – CECRI), Karaikudi 630003, Tamilnadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CSIR – Central Electrochemical Research Institute (CSIR – CECRI)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR), New Delhi 110025, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR)

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Electrodics and Electrocatalysis (EEC) Division, CSIR – Central Electrochemical Research Institute (CSIR – CECRI), Karaikudi, 630003, Tamilnadu, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CSIR – Central Electrochemical Research Institute (CSIR – CECRI)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a facile development of a bi-functional electrocatalyst for oxygen evolution reaction (OER) and oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) is reported. A composite material comprising of tiny particles of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) embedded into cobalt oxide (Co3O4) flakes is prepared by sodium borohydride reduction method and followed by annealing at 600 °C under inert atmosphere. Structural, morphological and crystalline features are analyzed using FESEM, TEM, HRTEM, XRD and XPS studies. Moreover, optical and fluorescence properties of N-GQDs are studied using UV–visible and fluorescence spectroscopic techniques. These studies clearly reveal and confirm the formation of a composite material. Further electrochemical characteristics toward OER and ORR are investigated by using linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) techniques. Compared to the individual entities of pure Co3O4 and N-GQDs alone, the electrocatalytic activity of N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite material is significantly higher towards ORR. Similarly, the same composite material is also used as an electrocatalyst for OER in 0.1 M KOH aqueous electrolyte and it exhibits a lower overpotential of 330 mV to obtain a current density of 10 mA/cm2 along with higher electrocatalytic activity and the reason is mainly attributed to the synergistic effect between N-GQDs and Co3O4. Thus, N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite material is demonstrated to be a high performance bi-functional electrocatalyst for ORR and OER.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Oxygen evolution reaction and oxygen reduction reaction

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical energy conversion through water splitting reaction has attracted enormous attention as the next generation energy storage system particularly in the fields of fuel cells and metal−air batteries because of the ever increasing energy demands. A great deal of efforts has been made in recent time to develop the alternative energy storage and energy conversion catalysts possessing high efficiency, low cost and environmentally benign [1–4]. Electrocatalysis associated with oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) is the heart of key renewable-energy technologies. These reactions are very much important for various energy conversion systems such as fuel cells and water splitting processes. However, both these reactions are kinetically sluggish especially the slow rate of kinetics is responsible for almost 15% energy loss because of its multistep proton−coupled electron transfer process, which is a critical issue for practical applications [5,6]. In order to resolve this problem, many researchers across the globe are working towards the development of materials to be employed as bi-functional electrocatalysts for efficient and cost−effective processes associated with ORR and OER. It is a viable alternative to accelerate the rate of such reactions [7]. Currently, the most widely used bi-functional electrocatalysts have precious metals like Pt, Ir, Ru and PtIrO2, and they are really expensive though the efficiency is demonstrated to be very high. In addition to high cost, the scarcity of these metals limits their commercial exploitation for the energy conversion systems [8–10]. Hence, aiming to avoid the usage of such precious metals and metal oxide catalysts (Pt [11], Ir [12], Ru [13], IrO2 [14], and RuO2 [15]), various non−precious materials have been synthesized and utilized as alternative electrocatalysts for both ORR and OER [16,17]. Primarily transition metal oxides, in particular single and complex oxides of Mn, Co and Ni have been shown to be an effective bi-functional catalyst towards ORR and OER, similar to that of precious metal catalysts in alkaline electrolyte [18–23]. Among the other low cost materials reported, cobalt oxides are capable of performing both ORR and OER effectively, due to good efficiency, earth abundance and easily made as an electrocatalyst. Moreover, this has largely been investigated as a stable electrocatalyst for OER in alkaline electrolyte [24,25]. For example, Su et al. reported the high surface area Co/NC composite material as a better bi-functional electrocatalyst, and the reason is attributed to the synergistic effect between carbon and Co nanoparticles (NPs) within the composite [26]. Jiang et al. studied Co3O4 nanoparticles grown onto nitrogen and boron doped graphene hollow microspheres and reported as a good bi-functional catalyst for OER and ORR [27]. Ganesan et al. examined cobalt sulfide nanoparticles anchored onto nitrogen and sulphur co-doped graphene oxide materials for both OER and ORR, and demonstrated to be a high performance bi-functional catalyst [28]. Liu et al. reported cubic Co3O4 chemically attached onto multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) composite material and proved as an outstanding bi-functional electrocatalyst [29]. Similarly Liang et al. studied Co3O4 nanoparticles embedded onto reduced graphene oxide that exhibit a high performance activity for both OER and ORR in alkaline medium [30].

Heteroatom doped graphene quantum dots (GQDs), particularly nitrogen doped carbon based fluorescent materials have attracted much attention because of their effective tuning of electronic and optical properties along with localized chemical features. GQDs show resemblance to that of graphene nanosheets which exhibit excellent characteristic features such as high surface area, abundant surface functional groups used for better surface grafting and the fast electron transfer via π–π conjugated network that helps in facile making of a composite and this could further enhance the catalytic activity of these materials. Moreover, N-doping to GQDs could effectively tune the intrinsic properties of such nanomaterials and improve the electrocatalytic properties more specifically for ORR [31–35].

Keeping this in mind, here, a composite material consisting of N-GQDs/Co3O4 has been prepared and its structural, morphological and crystalline features are characterized using a variety of spectroscopic and microscopic techniques. Moreover electrochemical characteristics of such a composite material toward OER and ORR in 0.1 M KOH aqueous solution are also studied. Synthesis of this composite material involves just a simple mixing of CoCl2 and N-GQDs treated with NaBH4 as a reducing agent and followed by annealing at 600 °C under Ar atmosphere for about 2 h (Scheme 1 ). Structure and morphology of the resultant composite are analyzed by using XRD, FESEM, HRTEM, XPS and then the electrocatalytic activity is investigated by using cyclic voltammetry (CV), linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) and chronoamperometry (CA) techniques. These studies clearly reveal that the resultant composite material is found to exhibit a better catalytic activity towards OER and ORR in alkaline medium and the observed effect is mainly attributed to the synergistic effect between Co3O4 and N-GQDs within the composite material. For comparison similar studies with pure Co3O4 and N-GQDs alone are also carried out.

Cobalt chloride (CoCl2) was purchased from Hi-media, Chennai, India. Sodium borohydride (NaBH4), citric acid and Nafion were obtained from Sigma Aldrich, Bangalore, India. Urea was procured from Alfa Aesar, Mumbai, India. Isopropanol was purchased from Merck, Bangalore, India. All other chemicals were used as received without any further purification unless otherwise mentioned. Millipore water having a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm was obtained from Milli-Q water purification system procured from Merck and it was used for the preparation of all aqueous solutions employed for the analytical studies.

Nitrogen doped GQDs (N-GQDs) were prepared by following a simple method reported earlier [33]. Initially, 1 g of citric acid and 0.2 g of urea were dissolved in 25 mL of distilled water to yield a uniform dispersion. Then the mixture was transferred into 100 mL Teflon autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. After that the reactor was cooled down to room temperature and a crude black coloured product was obtained. This product was further dissolved in 50 mL of water and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for about 15 min to remove the bigger sized particles. The settled precipitate was removed through multiple centrifugation process along with the brown colour supernatant solution that also separated out. The supernatant solution was further purified using dialysis membrane (12000 Da) in water for overnight. This clear solution was illuminated with UV light and observed to emit a strong blue colour fluorescence. Finally, the resultant solution was dried in an oven at 60 °C for 24 h to obtain the purified black coloured N-GQDs that is dispersible in water.

In a typical synthesis, 0.1 M of CoCl2 was dissolved in 10 mL of distilled water. To this solution, 5 mL of 2 mg of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) dispersed in water was added and then the mixture was vigorously stirred for about 10 min. Afterwards, 20 mL of 0.1 M solution of sodium borohydride (NaBH4) was added into the above mixture with constant stirring for about 15 min to obtain a colloidal dispersion. Then the resultant dispersion was allowed to dry at 60 °C for about 10 h. Finally, the resultant powder was annealed at 600 °C for about 2 h under Ar atmosphere and this procedure leads to the formation of a black coloured product namely, N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite material. Structural and morphological characteristics of this particular composite material were analyzed by using microscopic, spectroscopic and XRD techniques.

The catalytic loading of working electrode was performed using the following procedure. A catalytic ink was prepared with concentration of 5 mg of N-GQDs/Co3O4 powder dispersed in 1 mL of 1:1 v/v ratio of water/isopropanol containing 40 μL of 5 wt% Nafion solution and then the resultant mixture was ultrasonicated for about an hour to make a homogeneous ink solution. From this homogeneous ink solution, about 10 μL of catalytic ink was drop casted onto the surface of a pre-cleaned glassy carbon (GC) electrode (3 mm in diameter). Finally, N-GQDs/Co3O4 modified GC electrode was dried at room temperature and used as the working electrode for electrochemical measurements. All the electrochemical studies such as cyclic voltammetry (CV), linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) and chronoamperometry (CA) were carried out using a standard three−electrode configuration connected to Biologic Model: SP−240 workstation purchased from France. For investigating ORR features, this homogeneous ink was drop casted onto a rotating disk electrode (RDE) and the kinetic parameters were determined using rotating voltammetry studies. Thus, N-GQDs/Co3O4 modified rotating disc GC electrode was used as a working electrode, a Pt wire as the counter electrode and Ag/AgCl (sat. KCl) as the reference electrode respectively. Then all the potential values measured were converted into the potential values corresponding to reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE), according to Nernst equation given by, ERHE = EAg/AgCl + 0.059 × pH + 0.197. CV and LSV curves for ORR were recorded using a series of rotation speeds within the potential range of 0 V to −1.2 V vs. Ag/AgCl in O2 saturated 0.1 M KOH aqueous solution with the background being measured in N2 saturated 0.1 M KOH subtracted. Similarly CV, LSV and CA studies for OER were carried out within the potential range of 0 V–1.2 V vs. Ag/AgCl in 0.1 M KOH aqueous electrolyte. Tafel polarization studies were performed to determine the kinetic parameters associated with OER. All the other parameters are mentioned in the respective diagram.

TEM images were obtained using TECNAI G2 20 FEI model operated at 200 kW. TEM samples were prepared by drop casting method onto a copper grid. FTIR spectra were recorded using Bruker Optic GmbH TENSUR 27 model operated with a 162 software Opus version 6.5 m. Optical and fluorescence spectra were recorded using UV−VIS Perkin Elmer Lamda 650 and Infinite M200MPC model. XRD analysis was carried out using XPERT−PRO multipurpose X-ray diffractometer procured from The Netherlands, using Cu Kα radiation with a wavelength of 1.540 Å and 2θ values ranging from 10 to 90°. The surface morphology of these samples was observed through a VEGA3 SB TESCAN scanning electron microscope (SEM) and the elemental composition was analyzed using energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analyzer (X-MaxN) with a 20 mm2 SDD detector (Oxford Instruments, UK) with INCA software. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were collected using a Thermo Scientific MULTILAB 2000 base system with a twin anode of Mg/Al (300/400 W) X-ray source with the electron gun spot size of <50 μm. The obtained spectral lines were corrected using C 1s spectral line appeared at a binding energy value of 284.6 eV by curve fitting method and the background fittings were also performed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and microscopic studies of N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, N-GQDs are prepared through a simple bottom−up approach method by using citric acid as a carbon source and urea as the precursor for nitrogen dopant with the assistance of pyrolysis technique. Structural and morphological features of the resultant N-GQDs are analyzed by using transmission electron microscope (TEM) and the corresponding image is displayed in Fig. 1 A.

TEM image of N-GQDs reveals the formation of a well dispersed and uniform sized distribution of particles with an average diameter of 5 ± 0.3 nm. Inset image shows the formation of lattice fringe pattern having “d” spacing of 0.32 nm corresponding to (002) plane of the graphitic carbon associated with that of N-GQDs. These observations are in well agreement with the other reported works [36,37]. Further the optical properties of N-GQDs are analyzed using UV–visible and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopic studies. UV–visible absorption spectrum of N-GQDs shown in Fig. 1B (a) displays a typical absorption peak at 335 nm, which is designated to n−π* transition and another absorption hump appeared at 400 nm that corresponds to π−π* transition. Upon exposure to UV light, these N-GQDs display an intense blue colour fluorescence emission and the corresponding fluorescence spectra are shown in Fig. 1B (b). In order to understand the fluorescence behaviour of N-GQDs, PL spectra were recorded for different excitation wavelengths from 360 nm (a) to 470 nm (l) (Fig. 1B). The maximum fluorescence emission is observed at a wavelength of 495 nm for an excitation wavelength of 460 nm. It is noted that the fluorescence emission spectra show the excitation wavelength dependent emission behaviour and the peak position of emission wavelength is blue shifted with respect to change in the excitation wavelength. Such an excitation dependent emission behaviour is mainly attributed to the presence of abundant surface functional groups namely CO, CO and OCOH. Particularly these functional groups create different energy levels due to various surface states resulting in the fluorescence emission of N-GQDs that varies with the excitation wavelengths [38,39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological analyses of N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite
ce:section.ce:para: The composite material namely, N-GQDs/Co3O4 is prepared using a simple mixing of CoCl2 and N-GQDs treated with a reducing agent viz., NaBH4 and followed by annealing under inert atmosphere condition. Structural morphology of the resultant composite, N-GQDs/Co3O4 is analyzed by using field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) studies and the corresponding images are shown in Fig. 2 . FESEM images of N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite material recorded at low and high magnifications are shown in Fig. 2A and B. These images clearly depict the formation of beautiful flake-like (platelet) crystalline structures. It is very difficult to find out N-GQDs in these images, due to their very smaller size and hence, the obtained composite material exposes predominantly Co3O4 flakes. However, deeper analysis of these images shows the existence of very tiny particles of N-GQDs within these flakes. This is further confirmed by HRTEM images shown in Fig. 2C and D. It can be noted from these images that N-GQDs are incorporated as tiny particles into Co3O4 matrix that exhibits visible flake-like morphology. Moreover, the formation of a crystalline structure of the composite is confirmed by the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) image shown as an inset in Fig. 2C. Besides the formation of lattice fringe pattern these images also reveal the resultant composite possesses face centered cubic (fcc) crystalline structure. Fig. 2D depicts the lattice fringe pattern of N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite and from this image the crystalline lattice “d” spacing of 0.46 nm corresponding to the lattice plane of (111) spacing of Co3O4 is determined which is in agreement with the previous works on Co3O4 composite materials [40–43]. Similarly, the interplanar spacing of 0.32 nm corresponds to (002) lattice fringe plane of the graphitic carbon arises from N-GQDs is also identified. These results provide a strong evidence for the formation of fcc crystalline structure of N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite.

In addition, the lattice parameter values calculated from SAED (Supplementary information (SI); Fig. S1) pattern exactly matches with the XRD pattern of Co3O4 (JCPDS card No. 74-1657) [6,44]. Comparison of “d” spacing values from SAED pattern and the standard JCPDS card No. 74-1657 of pristine Co3O4 is provided in Table 1 . Furthermore, energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopic studies (EDX) (SI; Fig. S2) also suggest the formation of stoichiometric ratio of N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite. It is observed that the stoichiometric composition of Co (30.21%), O (52.42%) and the corresponding carbon peak from N-GQDs (N-GQDs content of ∼17.37%) is particularly determined for N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XRD and FTIR studies of N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite
ce:section.ce:para: Crystalline structure and phase purity of the resultant composite material are analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) studies and the corresponding results are shown in Fig. 3 A. XRD pattern of N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite shows six prominent peaks at 2θ values of 19.2, 31.2, 37.1, 45.1, 59.7 and 65.6 respectively and these XRD peaks can be indexed to the respective (111), (220), (311), (400), (511) and (440) planes of face-centered cubic (fcc) phase of pristine Co3O4 (Fd3m, JCPDS card No. 74-1657) [6,44]. All these diffraction peaks observed for this particular composite material matches very well with the standard data of pristine Co3O4 (Fd3m, JCPDS card No. 74-1657), and there is no extra or other peaks for impurities indicating the as-prepared N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite is highly pure and crystalline in nature. Generally, XRD peak of N-GQDs appears at the 2θ value of around 26.8° and is a very broad peak (SI; Fig. S3) corresponding to (002) diffraction plane of the graphitic carbon [36,37]. But, XRD pattern of N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite showed no peaks for N-GQDs and even the diffraction intensity values of other peaks are also lower and relatively very weak. This is due to a very low concentration of N-GQDs in the prepared composite material.

Furthermore, the presence of surface functional groups in the resultant composite is examined by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy and the corresponding spectra are shown in Fig. 3B. FTIR spectra of N-GQDs/Co3O4 shown in Fig. 3B (a) displayed peaks at 3429 cm−1 and 3189 cm−1 ascribed to the stretching vibrations of NH and OH groups of N-GQDs which suggest that N-GQDs are highly hydrophilic in nature. Similarly the formation of a peak observed at 2852 cm−1 is assigned to CH stretching frequency and similarly the peaks formed at 1663 cm−1 and at 1408 cm−1 are ascribed to the vibrational frequency of CO and COO− groups respectively. These vibrational peaks of NH, OH, CO and COO− can also be observed in the case of N-GQDs alone [Fig. 3B (c)], indicating that N-GQDs are incorporated over the Co3O4 flakes forming a crystalline composite structure. Moreover, N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite also shows two strong bands at 570 cm−1 and 662 cm−1 displaying a clear evidence for the presence of crystalline Co3O4. These two distinct peaks are originated from the stretching vibrations of cobalt – oxide bond. The first peak at 570 cm−1 is assinged to OB3 vibration in the spinel lattice where B denotes Co3+ in an octahedral holes and the second band at 662 cm−1 is attributed to ABO3 vibration where A denotes Co2+ in the tetrahedral hole. These similar vibration bands are also observed in case of Co3O4 alone displayed in Fig. 3B (b). Finally the peaks formed at 1600 cm−1 and 3600 cm−1 are assigned to OH stretching and bending modes of H2O respectively which may be adsorbed by the sample. These results clearly confirm the formation of N-GQDs/Co3O4composite [45–48].

Surface properties, chemical nature, elemental composition and valence state of N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite are further studied by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). XPS survey spectrum of N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite reveals the chemical composition of this material consisting of Co, C, O and N (SI; Fig. S4) and the corresponding high resolution scans of individual elements are displayed in Fig. 4 . High-resolution N 1s spectrum of N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite shown in Fig. 4A can be divided into three different peaks with binding energy values of 399 eV, 400 eV and 401.8 eV corresponding to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N respectively. These nitrogen species generally have a reasonable effect on the electrochemical performance, especially for ORR [23,49]. Fig. 4B displays the high resolution spectrum of C 1s showing the formation of four different types of C species namely CN, CC, CO and CO, indicating the existence of carbon atoms in different environment associated to N and O heteroatoms and the corresponding binding energy values are identified to be 284.7 eV, 285.1 eV, 286.2 eV and 288.4 eV respectively. Fig. 4C shows the high resolution spectrum of Co 2p and the peaks appeared at the binding energy values of 780.4 eV and 785.4 eV that can be assigned to Co 2p3/2 and its corresponding satellite peak. Similarly the peak at 795.5 eV in the spectrum corresponds to Co 2p1/2. Interestingly the spin orbital coupling value (separation between these two peaks) is calculated to be 15.1 eV indicating the surface is predominantly exposed to Co3+ species and the formation of satellite peaks are attributed to Co2+ oxidation state [41]. This further proves that some portion of Co3+ species is reduced to Co2+ species and this phenomenon creates new oxygen vacancies [50,51]. Moreover, the high-resolution O 1s spectrum shown in Fig. 4D could be fitted with three different peaks observed at 533.7 eV, 531.6 eV and 529.7 eV designated to hydroxyl, carboxyl groups and the oxygen related to cobalt (II) oxide respectively. These results clearly suggest the formation of N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic activity of N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite material towards OER
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the electrocatalytic activity of N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite material towards OER, linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) studies are carried out using a standard three electrode system in 0.1 M KOH aqueous solution at a scan rate of 5 mV/s within the potential range of 1.0 V–2.2 V vs. RHE as depicted in Fig. 5 . For comparison, many other control experiments and the reference materials are also tested under the same identical conditions, including Co3O4 (c), N-GQDs (e), bare GC (f), Pt/C (d) and IrO2 (a) respectively. Interestingly as-prepared N-GQDs/Co3O4 (b) composite material exhibits a lower onset potential (1.55 V vs. RHE) at a current density (j) of 10 mA/cm2 compared to the onset potential value of other samples like pure Co3O4 (1.72 V vs. RHE @ j = 10 mA/cm2), N-GQDs (1.80 V vs. RHE @ j = 0.5 mA/cm2), bare GC (1.82 V vs. RHE @ j = 0.1 mA/cm2) and commercial Pt/C (2.14 V vs. RHE @ j = 5 mA/cm2) respectively. On the other hand, when compared with the bench mark catalyst, IrO2 (1.46 V vs. RHE @ j = 10 mA/cm2), the onset potential of N-GQDs/Co3O4 (1.55 V vs. RHE) composite is slightly positive and displayed a lower overpotential value of 90 mV for OER. Basically the higher cost and scarcity of IrO2 limits its commercial applications. This lower onset potential value of N-GQDs/Co3O4 is mainly attributed to the better exposure of their active sites and the contact between N-GQDs/Co3O4 and GC surface, which can synergistically accelerate the reaction kinetics and reduce the thermodynamic energy barrier. Moreover, the potential required to obtain a current density of 10 mA/cm2 (which is equal to the solar energy conversion efficiency of 10%) is a most commonly used parameter to evaluate the OER activity of any catalyst [54]. The potential value of N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite to obtain the current density of 10 mA/cm2 is determined to be 330 mV which is very significant and almost similar to other best performing catalysts for OER [52–55]. Further, Tafel slope values are determined from the steady state current density measurements using CA studies performed at various applied potential values from 330 mV to 520 mV at the potential interval of 10 mV (Fig. 5C). These studies are carried out essentially to determine the efficiency of catalytic reaction in alkaline solution. Usually, Tafel slope is derived from the polarization curves by using Tafel equation, η = b log (j/j0), where η is the overpotential, b is the Tafel slope, j is the current density, and j0 is the exchange current density respectively. From these experiments, Tafel slope values are found to be 71, 98, 145 and 153 mV/dec for N-GQDs/Co3O4, Co3O4, bare GC and N-GQDs respectively and the corresponding linear Tafel plots are shown in Fig. 5B. Smaller value of Tafel slope (71 mV/dec) indicates the higher electrocatalytic activity of N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite material towards OER. In addition, the stability of this composite material is also investigated by CA technique at a constant operating potential of 1.53 V vs. RHE. Fig. 5D shows the current density of N-GQDs/Co3O4 is retained at 85% even after 10 h of measurement suggesting that the composite material possess a good stability in the alkaline medium. Similarly, the steady state current values for the state of art materials such as IrO2 and Pt/C are also determined by using CA techniques at various applied potentials and the corresponding Tafel slope (SI; Fig. S5) values are found to be 69 mV/dec and 195 mV/dec respectively. The resultant Tafel slope value of N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite suggests the favorable OER kinetics and the fast charge transfer process since the prepared material consisting of more active sites and strong electronic coupling between Co3O4 and N-GQDs.

Moreover, the catalytic performance of N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite is analyzed by determining the effective surface area. These values are calculated by measuring the non-faradaic capacitive current related with the double layer capacitance value (Cdl). These Cdl values are determined by recording CVs at various scan rates using N-GQDs/Co3O4 and Co3O4 modified electrodes in 0.1 M KOH electrolyte solution within the potential range of 0 V–0.1 V (SI; Fig. S6). The double layer capacitance (Cdl) values are calculated to be 0.28 mF/cm2 for N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite material and 0.0063 mF/cm2 for Co3O4 respectively. The larger Cdl value obtained for N-GQDs/Co3O4 indicates the higher electrocatalytic activity towards OER. Furthermore, turnover frequency (TOF) is also calculated for N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite by using the following equation [56,57].
(1)
TOF=
JS
4 Fm
where J (mA/cm2) is the measured current density from LSV curves, S is the surface area of glassy carbon electrode, the number 4 designates 4 electrons per mole of O2, F is Faraday's constant (96485 C/mol) and m is the number of moles of the active catalyst respectively. The parameter “m” is calculated by using the following equation, Γ = Q/nFA, where Q is the charge obtained from integration of the redox peak associated with Co3O4 at a fixed scan rate of 5 mV/s, n is the number of electrons transferred, F is Faraday's constant and A is the geometric area of the electrode respectively. From this equation, the surface concentration (Γ) or coverage value is calculated to be 4.58 × 10−8 mol/cm2. Then using these values TOF of N-GQDs/Co3O4 is found to be 4.2 s−1. This higher value of TOF points out to the higher catalytic activity towards OER. Compared to other previous reports this value is higher in case of cobalt based materials [56,57]. In general, these results vividly suggest the significant catalytic performance of N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite material and the reason is mainly attributed to the synergistic effect of Co3O4 and N-GQDs along with the large surface area value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic activity of N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite towards ORR
ce:section.ce:para: Similarly the electrocatalytic activity of the resultant N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite material is also explored for ORR applications. These studies are carried out by recording CV profiles within the potential range of 0 V to −1.2 V in O2 saturated 0.1 M KOH aqueous solution at a scan rate of 10 mV/s. The working electrode is prepared by simply drop casting the catalyst onto GC electrode and then the electrocatalytic activity for ORR is evaluated. For comparison N2 saturated solution was also tested for the background correction. The corresponding cyclic voltammograms recorded are shown in Fig. 6 . From the CVs displayed in Fig. 6A it can be seen that N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite material (b) showed a clear cathodic reduction peak at −0.47 V in presence of oxygen, whereas in case of N2 saturated solution (a) there is no obvious reduction peak implying that in presence of O2, N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite material modified electrode exhibits a pronounced catalytic activity towards ORR.

Particularly, the onset potential of as-prepared N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite material is found to be −0.30 V which is more positive potential at a current density of −0.1 mA/cm2 when compared with Co3O4 (−0.43 V @ j = −0.1 mA/cm2), N-GQDs (−0.46 V @ j = −0.1 mA/cm2) and bare GC (−0.49 V @ j = −0.08 mA/cm2) respectively. The significant change in the onset potential values and shift among the various materials stated above are clearly noted in Fig. 6B. In contrast, the onset potential value of the state of art Pt/C (−0.16 V @ j = −0.1 mA/cm2) is lower than that of N-GQDs/Co3O4. Even though Pt/C catalyst showed a higher catalytic activity towards ORR (SI; Fig. S7), the higher cost and availability of platinum limit its applications in the energy conversion devices. Furthermore, the kinetics of ORR is measured by using rotating disk electrode (RDE) for the composite material; N-GQDs/Co3O4 in O2 saturated 0.1 M KOH aqueous solution. LSV curves recorded at various rotating speeds from 100 rpm (b) to 2500 rpm (f) are shown in Fig. 6C. These curves clearly show that the reduction current density increased with increasing rotation speed because of the enhanced mass transfer and as a result of shortened diffusion distance at the electrode surface leading to significant enhancement in ORR with respect to increasing rotation speed. The corresponding Koutecky − Levich (KL) plots determined at different potential values for N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite material are displayed in Fig. 6D.

Interestingly KL plots displayed in Fig. 6D show a good linearity and almost constant slope values over the potential window from −0.4 V to −1 V which implies that the slope values follow the first order kinetics with respect to the concentration of dissolved O2. This could suggest that the number electrons transferred per O2 molecule is nearly same at various potential values during ORR. Therefore the kinetic and number of electrons transferred per O2 molecule for ORR is calculated from KL equations shown below [3,4,6,20,23,26,28–32].
(2)
1 / J
=
[
1 /
J L
]
+
[
1 /
J k
]
=
(
1 /
Bω
1 / 2
)
+
(
1 /
J k
)
(3)
B=0 .2nF
C 0
D
2 / 3
υ
− 1
/ 6
where J is the measured current density, Jk is the kinetic current density, and JL is the diffusion limited current density; ω is the electrode rotation rate, F is Faraday's constant (96,485 C mol−1), C0 is the bulk concentration of O2 (1.2 × 10−6 mol cm−3), D is the diffusion coefficient of O2 (1.9 × 10−5 cm2 s−1), and ν is the kinematic viscosity of the electrolyte (0.01 cm2 s−1) respectively. Here the constant 0.2 is used when the rotating rate is expressed in rpm [3,4,6,26]. From these equations, the “n” values are calculated to be 3.7 to 4.0 at the potential values of −0.4 V to −1.0 V for N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite material as depicted in Fig. 6D. The number of electrons transferred is also calculated at lower potential ranges at −0.2 V, −0.25 V, −0.3 V, −0.32 V and −0.35 V vs. Ag/AgCl, and the value of “n” is estimated to be 0.1, 0.4, 1.0, 1.6 and 2.8 respectively. These values suggest the incomplete reduction of O2 at lower overpotential values. These results vividly indicate that the resultant composite material N-GQDs/Co3O4 could be used as an efficient electrocatalyst for ORR and mostly proceeds through a four electron transfer process. Further, the potential use of this material for ORR is also examined in terms of the tolerance effect towards methanol crossover along with CO poisoning and these results are compared with commercial Pt/C catalyst. Chronoamperometric (i−t) measurements are carried out to evaluate these effects and methanol is introduced during the analysis; and the corresponding results are analyzed (SI; Fig. S7). The current response obtained for N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite material remains unchanged even after the addition of methanol indicating the excellent tolerance of this particular material to methanol crossover effect. But, in the case of Pt/C catalyst, a sudden jump in the current value upon addition of methanol is noticed thereby decreasing the catalytic activity since the active sites of platinum nanoparticles could be blocked by the adsorption of CO or other products formed due to methanol oxidation. Nevertheless these studies clearly show the excellent bi-functional electrocatalytic activity of N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite material towards OER and ORR in alkaline medium suggesting the possible use of this material in alkaline fuel cells.

In order to understand the higher electrocatalytic activity of N-GQDs/Co3O4 for both ORR and OER, the data analysis by correlating with the structure and morphology of the composite material is also performed. In general, pyridinic−N and graphitic−N doped carbon materials can reduce the overpotential (η) and increases the catalytic activity. The better catalytic properties of N-GQDs arise from the dopant nitrogen (N) due to the strong electro-negativity of N resulting in a strong electrostatic force between N and C which assists the O2 adsorption and dissociation on the adjacent carbon and thereby facilitates ORR [58]. Similarly, for OER the adjacent carbon atoms are more positive that may lead to possible adsorption of OH− ions and promotes the electron transfer to intermediates viz., O., OH. and OOH. [59]. In addition, transition metal oxide (Co3O4) composite with N-doped carbon materials noticeably increased both ORR and OER activity due to increased surface area value and the synergistic effect [60–68]. Moreover, the direct reduction by sodium borohydride (NaBH4) introduces significant increase in the oxygen vacancies sites, which in turn has effectively improved the catalytic activity for OER [61,62,66–68].

A new composite material, N-GQDs/Co3O4 has been successfully prepared through a simple NaBH4 reduction and one-pot annealing method. Structural, morphological and crystalline phase characteristics are analyzed by using FESEM, TEM, XPS and XRD studies. Results obtained from these techniques clearly show the formation of a composite material with flake-like structures possessing higher surface area values. The resultant composite material exhibited excellent bi-functional electrocatalytic activity towards OER and ORR in alkaline medium showing a lower onset potential, lower overpotential, higher current density values and good stability for these reactions. The superior electrocatalytic activity of N-GQDs/Co3O4 composite material could be mainly attributed to the following factors: (1) larger electrochemical surface area; (2) faster electron transfer kinetics; (3) strong electronic coupling between N-GQDs and Co3O4; (4) synergistic effect between N-GQDs and Co3O4; (5) heteroatom doped GQDs (N-GQDs) improved the catalytic activity for ORR; and finally (6) the direct reduction of cobalt salt using NaBH4 results in a large number of oxygen vacant sites on Co3O4 crystalline lattice which significantly enhanced the catalytic activity for OER. Hence, the proposed material could find potential application in alkaline fuel cells.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: AM and SVS are thankful to Council of Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR), India for providing Senior Research Fellowship (SRF) for pursuing Ph. D. degree program. Authors acknowledge Central Instrumentation Facility (CIF) of CSIR – CECRI, Karaikudi for providing necessary equipments for characterizations and analyses.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
mmc1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2017.11.157.
